---
title: 'Choosing the right pond: Spatial clustering of street graffiti by visual complexity'
bibliography: ../references.bib
csl: ../Drafts/apa.csl
output:
  word_document:
    reference_docx: ../drafts/20260318_Manuscript CVWB.docx
  html_document: default
  pdf_document: default
---

```{r manuscript-values, include=FALSE}
output_root <- ".."
output_candidates <- list.dirs(path = output_root, recursive = FALSE, full.names = FALSE)
output_candidates <- grep(
  "^[0-9]{8}_script_choosing_right_pond_outputs$",
  output_candidates,
  value = TRUE
)
if (length(output_candidates) == 0) {
  stop("No dated output folder found matching *_script_choosing_right_pond_outputs")
}
output_dir <- file.path(output_root, sort(output_candidates, decreasing = TRUE)[1])
results_path <- file.path(output_dir, "all_results.xlsx")
metadata_path <- file.path(output_dir, "manuscript_metadata.csv")

read_results <- function(sheet) {
  readxl::read_xlsx(results_path, sheet = sheet)
}

fmt_int <- function(x) {
  format(round(as.numeric(x)), big.mark = ",", scientific = FALSE, trim = TRUE)
}

fmt_num <- function(x, digits = 1) {
  formatC(as.numeric(x), format = "f", digits = digits, big.mark = ",")
}

fmt_pct <- function(x, digits = 2) {
  formatC(as.numeric(x), format = "f", digits = digits)
}

fmt_p <- function(x) {
  sub("0\\.", ".", as.character(x))
}

fmt_p_value <- function(x) {
  sub("^0\\.", ".", formatC(as.numeric(x), format = "f", digits = 3))
}

obs_details <- read_results("Observation Details")
segment_geometry <- read_results("Segment Geometry Summary")
main_graffiti_summary <- read_results("Main Graffiti Summary")
raw_graffiti_summary <- read_results("Raw Graffiti Summary")
graffiti_stats <- read_results("Graffiti Summary Stats")
mhi_main <- read_results("MHI Results (3 types, FF)")
mhi_other <- read_results("MHI Results (4 types, FF)")
misclass_summary <- read_results("Misclass Sens Summary")
metadata <- read.csv(metadata_path, stringsAsFactors = FALSE)
metadata_value <- function(key) {
  metadata$value[metadata$key == key][1]
}

permutation_cache_file <- list.files(
  file.path(output_dir, "cache"),
  pattern = "^df_permutated_MHI_3types_FF_.*iter\\.rds$",
  full.names = TRUE
)[1]
permuted_main <- readRDS(permutation_cache_file)

obs_count <- function(label) {
  obs_details$Count[obs_details$Label == label][1]
}

summary_count <- function(type) {
  main_graffiti_summary$n[main_graffiti_summary$graffiti_types_grouped == type][1]
}

summary_pct <- function(type) {
  graffiti_stats$percentage[graffiti_stats$graffiti_types_grouped == type][1]
}

raw_count <- function(type) {
  raw_graffiti_summary$n[raw_graffiti_summary$graffiti_types_grouped == type][1]
}

geometry_value <- function(column) {
  segment_geometry[[column]][segment_geometry$Sample == "Analytic focal segments"][1]
}

mhi_log <- mhi_main[mhi_main$`Log Transformations` == "Log Transformation + 1", ]
mhi_other_log <- mhi_other[mhi_other$`Log Transformations` == "Log Transformation + 1", ]

mhi_value <- function(pair, column, data = mhi_log) {
  data[[column]][data$`Graffiti Types` == pair][1]
}

mhi_difference <- function(pair) {
  observed <- mhi_value(pair, "Observed MHI Value")
  expected <- mhi_value(pair, "Mean Permutated MHI")
  round((expected - observed) / expected * 100)
}

all_main_p <- fmt_p(unique(mhi_log$`P Value`)[1])
all_other_p <- fmt_p(unique(mhi_other_log$`P Value`)[1])
max_misclass_p <- max(misclass_summary$`Max P Value`, na.rm = TRUE)
n_permutations <- nrow(permuted_main) /
  (dplyr::n_distinct(permuted_main$`Log Transformations`) *
    dplyr::n_distinct(permuted_main$`Graffiti Types`))
r_version <- as.character(getRversion())
vegan_version <- as.character(utils::packageVersion("vegan"))
data_collection_period <- metadata_value("data_collection_period")
reported_min_p <- fmt_p(metadata_value("reported_min_p"))
alpha_05 <- fmt_p(metadata_value("alpha_05"))

table1 <- graffiti_stats |>
  dplyr::transmute(
    `Graffiti Types` = graffiti_types_grouped,
    Min = min,
    Max = max,
    Mean = fmt_num(mean, 2),
    SD = fmt_num(sd, 2),
    Total = fmt_int(total),
    Percentage = fmt_pct(percentage, 2)
  )
```

# Abstract

Graffiti research shows that writers seek visible locations, but less is known about whether the graffiti already present at a location shapes the co-presence of different graffiti types. We extend spot theory by examining whether graffiti types with contrasting levels of visual complexity are spatially sorted across street segments. We assess whether masterpieces, tags, and intermediate types co-present less often than expected under indifference. Using systematic social observation across `r fmt_int(obs_count("Street Segments with at least One Classified Graffiti Type"))` street segments in Ghent, Belgium, we find that co-presence is significantly lower than expected, in particular between masterpieces and tags, the most visually contrasting types. Our findings indicate that graffiti location choice is not only about finding visible places, but also about the competitive visual composition of those places.

**Keywords**: Graffiti, Spot Theory, Visual Complexity, Visibility, Competition, Location Choice

> *"Graffiti is not just art - it is also sport, and the fields of this sporting competition are the spots where graffiti is written."* [@ferrell2010, p. 50]

# Introduction

Graffiti writers do not choose locations randomly. They choose places where other people can see their work. Visibility matters because recognition, reputation, and status depend on being seen [@ferrell2010; @kindynis2018; @macdonald2001]. Earlier research shows that writers prefer locations with large potential audiences [@campos2013; @ferrell2010; @kindynis2018; @macdonald2001; @vanloon2014]. Spot theory explains this as a choice made after weighing visibility, location, and risk [@ferrell2010]. Ethnographic studies also describe good spots as exposed and visually prominent places [@campos2013; @kindynis2018; @macdonald2001; @vanloon2014]. Quantitative studies also confirms that graffiti concentrates in locations with high public exposure [@kuralarasan2024; @megler2014; @tokuda2021].

Most studies discuss visibility as exposure to potential audiences [@campos2013; @ferrell2010; @kindynis2018; @macdonald2001; @vanloon2014]. We claim that visibility may also depend on what is already present at the location. When several visual objects are in the same place, they compete for attention [@brighenti2010]. Writers look for surfaces that are noticeable within a visual field [@kindynis2018]. A busy street can therefore be a poor spot if existing graffiti draws attention away from new work. Research also shows that the surrounding visual environment can affect whether graffiti is noticed [@craw2006]. Writers may use size, colour, and complexity to stand out from other graffiti [@macdonald2001]. Location choice therefore involves two questions: whether a location is visible to an audience, and whether the graffiti can remain noticeable among the graffiti already present there -- a question that depends on differences in visual complexity between graffiti types.

Graffiti types differ in visual complexity and execution demands. We use visual complexity to refer to observable features such as size, detail, colour use, and the effort needed to produce the work. Tags are graphic name inscriptions that are usually made quickly [@dovey2012; @kindynis2018]. Pieces or masterpieces are larger and more elaborate. They usually require planning, skill, time, and colour use [@dovey2012; @halsey2006; @macdonald2001]. Stencils, illustrations, throw-ups, and slogans fall between tags and masterpieces in detail and visual dominance [@dovey2012; @kindynis2018]. These differences matter because existing graffiti can affect the visibility of new graffiti in the same street segment. We therefore examine whether graffiti types with different levels of visual complexity appear together in the same street segments, or whether they are found in different street segments.

We focus on non-gang graffiti. This distinction matters because gang graffiti and non-gang graffiti may follow different spatial logics. Gang graffiti is often linked to territorial marking. Non-gang graffiti is more closely linked to visibility, recognition, and name circulation [@bloch2020; @ferrell2010; @ley1974; @macdonald2001]. This focus fits Ghent. Gang-related graffiti was not observed in the study area. @kuralarasan2024 also reported that gang-related graffiti activities do not exist in Ghent and were stopped more than a decade ago. In the rest of this article, graffiti refers to non-gang graffiti.

Spot theory identifies four criteria in graffiti spot selection: audiences and visibility, longevity and durability, availability and competition, and seriality and accumulation [@ferrell2010]. These criteria explain why writers seek exposed locations, durable surfaces, available spots, and places that support reputation. Yet spot theory says less about the visual composition of graffiti already present at a location. We extend this point by moving from absolute visibility to relative visibility. Absolute visibility means that a location exposes graffiti to potential audiences. Relative visibility means that graffiti remains noticeable in relation to the graffiti already present at the same location. If this argument is correct, graffiti types with contrasting levels of visual complexity should be less likely to be co-present in the same street segments than expected under spatial indifference.

We test this argument with systematic graffiti observations from Ghent, Belgium. We use street segments as the spatial unit because graffiti within the same segment can be seen as part of the same local visual environment. We use the Morisita-Horn Index to measure co-presence between graffiti types. We then compare observed MHI values with values from a Fixed-Fixed null model. This allows us to test whether visually contrasting graffiti types share street segments less often than expected under spatial indifference.

This study contributes to spot theory by extending visibility from absolute visibility to relative visibility. Previous research has mainly examined visibility as exposure to potential audiences [@campos2013; @ferrell2010; @kindynis2018; @macdonald2001; @vanloon2014]. The present study examines whether visibility depends not only on exposure to potential audiences (absolute visibility), but also on the position of a graffiti item relative to other graffiti already present at the same location (relative visibility). In doing so, the study extends spot theory by incorporating competitive positioning within the visual environment. The study also makes a methodological contribution. It examines co-presence between graffiti types with the Morisita-Horn Index and compares observed MHI estimates with Fixed-Fixed null-model estimates. This complements quantitative graffiti studies that examine clustering or graffiti counts by shifting attention to type co-presence within street segments [@kuralarasan2024; @megler2014; @tokuda2021].

# Theory and hypotheses

The theoretical problem is not whether graffiti writers value visibility, but how visibility operates once a street segment already contains graffiti. Spot theory treats visibility as a central condition of spot selection [@ferrell2010], but it mainly frames visibility as exposure to potential audiences [@ferrell2010; @vanloon2014]. We extend this argument from absolute visibility to relative visibility. Absolute visibility refers to whether a location exposes graffiti to viewers; relative visibility refers to whether a particular graffiti item remains noticeable in relation to surrounding graffiti. The same street segment can therefore provide high audience exposure and still be an unfavourable setting for a particular graffiti item if surrounding work draws attention away from it [@brighenti2010; @craw2006].

This distinction matters because graffiti types differ competitively, not only descriptively. They differ in visual complexity and execution demands [@dovey2012]. They also differ in how strongly they can dominate a visual field [@macdonald2001; @kindynis2018]. Elaborate work can draw attention away from simpler forms nearby [@craw2006]. These differences also relate to graffiti careers and status. Writers may move from frequent tagging to more elaborate forms as skill, experience, and status increase [@lachmann1988; @macdonald2001]. This does not mean that graffiti types must separate in space. It means that the surrounding type composition can matter. Taggers may be judged by reach, frequency, and placement [@kindynis2018; @macdonald2001]. Piece writers are more often judged by style, effort, and complexity [@lachmann1988; @macdonald2001].

Recognition is type-specific, so other writers form an important peer audience. They do not only see the work; they evaluate style, competence, risk, and authenticity [@kindynis2018; @macdonald2001]. Writers mainly seek recognition from other writers, while the general public is a secondary audience [@ferrell2010; @kindynis2018]. The value of a location is therefore relational: it depends on the graffiti against which the work is compared [@brighenti2010; @haworth2013]. A tag placed among other tags can compete with direct rivals. The same tag placed among visually dominant masterpieces may be harder to see, even when the street segment attracts many viewers [@craw2006]. It may also be judged by a different status standard, because muralists and taggers seek different types of fame [@lachmann1988]. An elaborate piece may likewise be evaluated among other complex works rather than among forms that follow different status criteria [@macdonald2001].

This peer-evaluation logic clarifies spot theory's competition criterion. Writers are not only competing for visible locations. They are also competing for attention within visual environments [@brighenti2010] that already contain graffiti [@haworth2013]. Spot theory recognises that writers assess competition at a spot [@ferrell2010]. This has also been described as competition for visual space and exposure [@bloch2020]. Here, competition refers to the graffiti already present in the street segment. Existing graffiti may make a segment favourable or unfavourable for new work. Similar graffiti types can provide a clearer competitive setting because writers are judged against relevant rivals. Taggers are evaluated mainly through quantity and placement. Piece writers are evaluated more through artistic quality, technique, and complexity [@macdonald2001]. Contrasting and visually dominant work can make a segment less favourable in two ways. First, it can make simpler forms harder to see [@craw2006]. Second, it can place the new work against an incompatible status standard [@lachmann1988]. If writers respond to these conditions, graffiti types should not be co-present at random.

Frank's relative-status framework helps explain why the comparison group matters. A male frog may do better in a pond where it is large relative to other males than in a pond where most competitors are larger [@frank1985]. Applied to graffiti, a writer may gain more recognition by placing a tag in a segment dominated by other tags than in a segment dominated by masterpieces. In the first setting, the tag competes with direct rivals. In the second, the tag may be harder to see because elaborate work dominates the visual field [@craw2006]. This is the relative-visibility mechanism. A graffiti item must remain noticeable and favourably positioned in relation to surrounding graffiti. @craw2006 provide a useful example. After murals were installed on one wall of an alley, tagging declined on that wall but appeared on the opposite wall. This suggests that writers may move within the same local setting when one surface becomes visually unfavourable.

If writers make these comparisons repeatedly, the pattern should appear at the street-segment level. Street segments with visually comparable graffiti should provide more favourable competitive settings. Street segments dominated by contrasting types should provide less favourable settings. This expectation is consistent with niche-partitioning theory, where competitors reduce overlap in shared resource use under competitive pressure [@jung2015; @pianka1974; @sale1974; @stewart2002]. Applied to graffiti, wall space is still contested. But writers do not claim it only as physical territory. They use it to secure a "territory of shared attention" [@brighenti2010] and to increase visibility and exposure [@vandeviver2025]. Taggers also show individual target preferences, including a preference for targets that already contain graffiti [@haworth2013; @vandeviver2025]. Placement is therefore not random. This leads to our hypothesis. Writers seek recognition, and recognition depends on visibility. Visibility depends on audience exposure, but also on the graffiti already present in the same street segment. If existing graffiti is visually comparable, the segment may provide a favourable setting. If existing graffiti is visually dominant or judged by different standards, the segment may be less favourable. We therefore hypothesize that graffiti types with contrasting levels of visual complexity are less likely to co-present in the same street segments than expected under spatial indifference.

# Data^[The observation data and analysis scripts supporting this study are available in an online repository; URL omitted for blind review.]

We investigated graffiti in the city centre of Ghent, Belgium. We limited the study area to the city centre because it has many graffiti instances and could be observed systematically. Inner-city areas are useful for graffiti research because they contain diverse spatial settings and different forms of graffiti management [@haworth2013; @kuralarasan2024]. This area therefore provided a manageable site with enough graffiti to study type co-presence. We collected the data in `r data_collection_period` using systematic social observation (SSO). SSO is often used to measure community disorder and the physical features of places [@sampson2004; @sas2022]. It also captures details that administrative data often miss [@bloch2020]. We used a structured observation form to classify graffiti across street segments in Ghent (Appendix 1). A team of `r fmt_int(obs_count("Total Observers"))` undergraduate students collected the data. They followed a one-month training programme. During training, they used graffiti photographs to learn how to classify type, scale, colour complexity, execution mode, readability, and dimensions. Each graffiti instance was coded by one observer. We discuss classification error in the Limitations section.


# Units of Analysis

We used street segments to aggregate graffiti instances. This follows research that uses street segments or similar small units to measure crime and disorder [@groff2010; @weisburd2004]. A street segment includes both sides of a street between intersections. In this analysis, the unit is operational. Each graffiti instance was assigned to a street-segment identifier. We then calculated co-presence from graffiti-type counts within those identifiers. This unit fits the argument because graffiti in the same street segment can plausibly be part of the same local visual environment. Graffiti around the corner is less likely to be seen together. The street-segment layer was polygon-based rather than midline-based. The statistical analysis does not use polygon area or shape directly. The polygons are used to define observation units in Ghent's irregular street network and to distinguish standard street segments, intersections, and bridges (see Appendix 2). The co-presence analysis then uses counts of graffiti types per street segment.

We collapsed the six observed graffiti types into three analytical groups: masterpieces, tags, and SITS. SITS combines stencils, illustrations, throw-ups, and slogans. The observation form records graffiti type, medium, number of colours, dimensions, surface, and readability (Appendix 1). This grouping creates a trade-off. It increases the visual contrast used in the hypothesis test, but it reduces detail on specific subtypes. We therefore repeated the analysis with the original six graffiti types.

Ghent city centre contains `r fmt_int(obs_count("Total Street Segments"))` street segments. We observed `r fmt_int(obs_count("Observed Street Segments"))` segments; `r fmt_int(obs_count("Not Observed Street Segments"))` were not observed. We excluded `r fmt_int(obs_count("Zero Graffiti Street Segments"))` segments with no graffiti. We also excluded `r fmt_int(obs_count("Street Segments Only with Other"))` segments that contained only graffiti coded as "Other" (see Appendix 3). These segments did not contain graffiti that could be assigned to the six predefined categories in the observation form. We also checked whether excluding "Other" affected the findings by repeating the analysis with "Other" as a fourth category. Appendix 3 reports observation status by street segment.

After these exclusions, the final dataset includes `r fmt_int(summary_count("Total"))` graffiti instances across `r fmt_int(obs_count("Street Segments with at least One Classified Graffiti Type"))` street segments (see Appendix 3). All three analytical graffiti types occur in the dataset. This is needed to test competition between types. The MHI analysis measures how strongly any two types share the same street segment. All inferential claims are therefore conditional on graffiti-active street segments with at least one classifiable graffiti instance. The analytic street segments are spatially extensive. Their mean length is `r fmt_num(geometry_value("Mean Length (m)"), 1)` m and their mean area is `r fmt_num(geometry_value("Mean Area (m2)"), 1)` m^2^ (see Appendix 4). Each street segment therefore typically contains several writable surfaces. Table 1 presents the descriptive statistics of the three graffiti types per street segment. Street-segment geometry by sample is reported in Appendix 4.

**Table 1**

*Numbers of graffiti per street segment by visual complexity. N = `r fmt_int(obs_count("Street Segments with at least One Classified Graffiti Type"))` street segments.*

```{r table1, echo=FALSE, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE}
flextable::flextable(table1) |>
  flextable::font(fontname = "Times New Roman", part = "all") |>
  flextable::fontsize(size = 12, part = "all") |>
  flextable::bold(part = "header") |>
  flextable::align(align = "center", part = "all") |>
  flextable::align(j = "Graffiti Types", align = "left", part = "all") |>
  flextable::border_remove() |>
  flextable::hline_top(border = officer::fp_border(width = 1), part = "all") |>
  flextable::hline_bottom(border = officer::fp_border(width = 1), part = "header") |>
  flextable::hline_bottom(border = officer::fp_border(width = 1), part = "body") |>
  flextable::autofit()
```


# Analytical Strategy

Our analytical approach draws on ecology, where researchers study co-presence and competition between species [@gotelli1996]. The Morisita-Horn Index (MHI) is often used to measure how strongly different species occur in the same habitats [@horn1966; @krebs1989]. We use the same logic here. The hypothesis concerns co-presence between graffiti types within street segments. It is not only about the total number of graffiti items or the clustering of one type. We treat street segments as graffiti habitats. The MHI measures how often each pair of graffiti types is present in the same street segments. The three pairs are masterpiece-tag, masterpiece-SITS, and SITS-tag. For two graffiti types x and y, the MHI is calculated as follows:

$$MHI_{xy} = \frac{2\sum_{i=1}^{n} P_{xi}\, P_{yi}}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} P_{xi}^2 + \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_{yi}^2} \tag{1}$$

In Equation 1, $MHI_{xy}$ measures the joint presence of graffiti type x and graffiti type y. These types can be masterpiece, SITS, or tag. $P_{xi}$ is the number of graffiti items of type x as a proportion of all graffiti items in street segment i. $P_{yi}$ is the same proportion for type y. The term n is the total number of street segments. The MHI ranges from 0 to 1 [@horn1966]. A value of 1 means complete co-presence. Both types are always present in the same street segment. A value of 0 means no co-presence. The two types are never present in the same street segment.

The distribution of graffiti types is uneven. Tags account for `r fmt_pct(summary_pct("Tag"), 2)` percent of the observations, while masterpieces account for `r fmt_pct(summary_pct("Masterpiece"), 2)` percent. Uneven distributions can affect the MHI because frequent types have more influence. To reduce this influence, researchers often transform observed frequencies before calculating overlap measures [@horn1966; @wolda1981]. We therefore applied a log (x + 1) transformation to the graffiti counts per street segment before computing MHI values.

We assessed statistical significance by comparing observed MHI values with null distributions from `r fmt_int(n_permutations)` Monte Carlo permutations. We used the Fixed-Fixed (FF) null model [@gotelli2000]. In this model, two quantities are fixed. The first is the total number of graffiti items per street segment. The second is the total count of each graffiti type across all street segments. Only the assignment of graffiti types to segments is randomised. Differences in segment activity and type prevalence therefore do not drive the null distribution. The FF specification is conservative because it preserves these marginal totals. Significant departures therefore reflect how types are arranged across street segments. Simulation benchmarks in ecology show that fixed-fixed algorithms have strong Type I control and can retain power under noisy matrices [@gotelli2000]. These benchmarks were developed for other co-occurrence indices, but they support FF as a conservative null framework [@gotelli2000].

Because the hypothesis predicts lower co-presence, we used one-tailed *p*-values. We calculated them as the proportion of permuted MHI values less than or equal to the observed MHI. If no permuted MHI was lower than or equal to the observed MHI, we reported *p* `r reported_min_p`. Under our hypothesis, low observed MHI values mean that two graffiti types share street segments less often than expected by chance. Similar permutation approaches are used in quantitative criminology [@chalfin2022; @groff2010] and have recently been applied to non-random target selection in graffiti [@vandeviver2025]. We conducted all analyses in R (version `r r_version`) [@Rcore]. We computed MHI values with the `vegan` package (version `r vegan_version`) [@vegan]. We also conducted sensitivity checks. These checks assessed whether the main conclusion changed under alternative transformations, local spatial restrictions, different type groupings, inclusion of "Other", and plausible classification error. Full details are reported in the appendices.

# Results

We examine whether graffiti types with contrasting levels of visual complexity are co-present in the same street segments less often than expected under spatial indifference. We therefore test whether the observed MHI for each pair is lower than the Fixed-Fixed null expectation. Figure 1 shows this comparison for masterpiece-tag, masterpiece-SITS, and SITS-tag.

![Figure 1: Observed and expected MHI values for masterpieces, SITS, and tags. MHI ranges from 0 to 1; lower values indicate lower co-presence. Histograms show Fixed-Fixed permutation distributions, and dashed vertical lines show observed MHI values.](`r file.path(output_dir, "Figure_1.png")`)


The upper panel reports low masterpiece-tag co-presence. The observed MHI is `r fmt_num(mhi_value("Masterpiece vs Tag", "Observed MHI Value"), 3)`, while the mean permuted MHI is `r fmt_num(mhi_value("Masterpiece vs Tag", "Mean Permutated MHI"), 3)`. No permuted MHI was below the observed MHI (*p* `r fmt_p(mhi_value("Masterpiece vs Tag", "P Value"))`). The observed MHI is about `r fmt_int(mhi_difference("Masterpiece vs Tag"))`% below the null mean. Masterpiece-tag co-presence is lower than expected under spatial indifference.

The middle panel reports masterpiece-SITS co-presence. The observed MHI is `r fmt_num(mhi_value("Masterpiece vs SITS", "Observed MHI Value"), 3)`, and the mean permuted MHI is `r fmt_num(mhi_value("Masterpiece vs SITS", "Mean Permutated MHI"), 3)`. The observed MHI is below the null expectation (*p* `r fmt_p(mhi_value("Masterpiece vs SITS", "P Value"))`) and about `r fmt_int(mhi_difference("Masterpiece vs SITS"))`% below the null mean. Masterpiece-SITS co-presence is lower than expected.

The bottom panel reports tag-SITS co-presence. The observed MHI is `r fmt_num(mhi_value("Tag vs SITS", "Observed MHI Value"), 3)`, and the mean permuted MHI is `r fmt_num(mhi_value("Tag vs SITS", "Mean Permutated MHI"), 3)`. The observed MHI is below the null expectation (*p* `r fmt_p(mhi_value("Tag vs SITS", "P Value"))`) and about `r fmt_int(mhi_difference("Tag vs SITS"))`% below the null mean. Tags and SITS therefore appear together less often than expected by chance. Among the three pairs, this pair is closest to the null expectation.

Across all three pairwise comparisons, observed co-presence is below the Fixed-Fixed null expectation (all *p* `r all_main_p`). This supports the hypothesis. Graffiti types with contrasting levels of visual complexity are co-present less often than expected under spatial indifference. The order also fits the argument. The difference from the null mean is largest for masterpiece-tag (`r fmt_int(mhi_difference("Masterpiece vs Tag"))`% below the null mean). It is smallest for SITS-tag (`r fmt_int(mhi_difference("Tag vs SITS"))`% below the null mean). This pattern is consistent with sorting by visual complexity, although we consider alternative explanations in the Discussion.

We conducted five sensitivity checks. The lower co-presence pattern remained stable under alternative count transformations (Appendix 5), spatially constrained permutations (Appendix 6), the six-type analysis (Appendix 7), inclusion of "Other" as a fourth category (Appendix 8), and simulated classification error (Appendix 9). The six-type check was the only partial exception. The overall pattern remained stable, but the masterpiece-throw-up pair was not significant under square-root and log transformations. Retaining the `r fmt_int(raw_count("Others"))` instances coded as 'Other' as a fourth analytic category produced the same main conclusion: all three main pairwise comparisons remained lower than expected (all *p* `r all_other_p`; Appendix 8). In the misclassification sensitivity analysis, co-presence remained lower than expected in all re-estimations. The largest p-value across the misclassification checks was `r fmt_p_value(max_misclass_p)`. These checks indicate that the main finding is not driven by one modelling choice.

# Discussion

We examined whether graffiti types with contrasting levels of visual complexity are co-present in the same street segments less often than expected under spatial indifference. Across `r fmt_int(obs_count("Street Segments with at least One Classified Graffiti Type"))` street segments in Ghent, observed co-presence was lower than the Fixed-Fixed null expectation for all three pairwise comparisons. The results support the hypothesis that visually contrasting graffiti types are less likely to share street segments than expected under the null model.

The main theoretical contribution is to extend spot theory from absolute visibility to relative visibility. Spot theory emphasizes that writers seek visible locations [@ferrell2010]. Prior research shows that graffiti concentrates in high-exposure areas [@kuralarasan2024; @megler2014; @tokuda2021]. Our findings suggest that visibility is not only a characteristic of the location. It also depends on the graffiti already visible in the same street segment. If location choice depended only on absolute visibility, highly exposed street segments would contain a broader mix of types. Instead, co-presence is below the null expectation for all three pairs. The order also follows visual complexity. The result is strongest for masterpiece-tag and weakest for tag-SITS. This suggests that writers may consider not only whether a place is visible, but also whether their work can stand out among existing graffiti.

These pairwise results should be read carefully. Masterpieces are rare. The absolute MHI for any pair involving masterpieces is therefore constrained by rarity as well as by competitive sorting. The appendix checks show that this pattern remains stable across alternative specifications. The Fixed-Fixed specification keeps both segment totals and global type totals constant [@gotelli2000]. Inference is therefore based on how types are arranged across street segments, not on marginal frequencies alone.

Two alternative explanations are important. First, subcultural overwriting norms may reduce co-presence between contrasting types. The norm is directional. Placing a lower-complexity type over a visually dominant piece is treated as a serious violation [@brighenti2010; @vanloon2014]. The reverse does not carry the same offence [@macdonald2001]. Second, visual overshadowing may discourage simpler forms from being placed on the same surface as visually dominant work. Simpler marks can become harder to notice next to elaborate compositions. Both mechanisms are plausible at the surface level. Our unit, however, is the street segment. The analytic street segments average `r fmt_num(geometry_value("Mean Length (m)"), 1)` m in length and `r fmt_num(geometry_value("Mean Area (m2)"), 1)` m^2^ in area. They usually contain many wall faces, poles, shutters, and other surfaces. A writer who respects the overwriting norm would avoid the wall bearing a masterpiece. There is less reason to avoid every other surface in the same street segment. @craw2006 make this distinction clear. Murals installed on one alley wall displaced tags to the opposite wall of the same alley. This shows relocation within a segment, not full segment-level separation. For this reason, surface-level mechanisms are unlikely to explain the full street-segment pattern by themselves.

The results are consistent with a competition-based interpretation, but they do not isolate one behavioural mechanism. The aggregate pattern shows reduced co-presence between contrasting types. It does not show whether this comes from active avoidance, type-specific attraction, surface opportunity, differential removal, or the sequence in which graffiti was produced. These explanations can operate together. Normative sorting, physical coverage, and competitive placement may each contribute to the pattern across the `r fmt_int(obs_count("Street Segments with at least One Classified Graffiti Type"))` street segments. Longitudinal data on placement sequence would be needed to separate these mechanisms. The present cross-sectional design does not provide such data.

The relative-visibility interpretation fits the recognition logic of graffiti writing. Writers seek recognition mainly from other writers. The general public is a secondary audience [@ferrell2010; @kindynis2018]. Peer recognition is also type-specific. Taggers are evaluated through reach and frequency. Piece writers are evaluated through style and technical quality [@kindynis2018; @macdonald2001]. A tag in a street segment dominated by masterpieces is therefore less favourable for two reasons. First, other taggers may be less concentrated in that setting. Second, piece writers use evaluative criteria that do not reward quantity-based tagging [@lachmann1988]. Public attention may also be drawn toward visually dominant work, reducing the exposure of simpler forms [@brighenti2010]. Placement in a visually comparable context may therefore improve recognition from the relevant audience. Taggers show a related preference by selecting targets that already contain tags [@haworth2013; @vandeviver2025]. This reflects a "right pond" mechanism [@frank1985], where writers seek settings in which they compare favourably with a specific peer group.

The practical implication is direct. Graffiti management should not rely only on total graffiti counts. A street segment with many tags is not the same as a street segment with a few masterpieces. These settings may attract different writers. They may also respond differently to removal or legal-wall policies. Removing tags from a tag-dominated segment may change the local peer environment and move tagging to similar nearby surfaces. A legal wall that attracts elaborate pieces may also change the type composition around it. Existing evidence is compatible with relocation and form shifts under targeted control [@craw2006; @haworth2013; @kindynis2018]. Management strategies should therefore consider both the number of graffiti items and the composition of graffiti types.

These findings are conditional on Ghent. The local graffiti culture is style-based, and visibility and recognition are central goals [@kindynis2018; @kuralarasan2024]. The logic may differ in places where gang graffiti and territorial control are more important [@bloch2019; @ley1974]. Our claims also apply only to graffiti-active street segments with at least one classifiable instance.

# Limitations and future directions

We infer competition from the spatial pattern. The results show that contrasting graffiti types are co-present less often than expected at the street-segment level. This fits the relative-visibility argument, but it does not show what individual writers intended when they chose a spot. Because the present research uses a cross-sectional design (`r data_collection_period`), we observe the pattern at one point in time. We cannot tell whether writers were actively avoiding certain street segments during the observation period, or whether the pattern was created earlier and remained in place. Ecology refers to this problem as the "ghost of competition past" [@connell1980; @gotelli1996; @stewart2002]. The FF framework controls for differences in type frequency and street-segment activity. It cannot show when the pattern emerged or whether writers still maintain it. Future research should combine interviews with direct ethnographic observations, such as travelling with writers. This would help reconstruct recent spot-selection choices. Repeated observations of the same street segments would also help test whether the pattern changes over time.

A second limitation concerns surface preferences. The FF null model controls for the total count of each type per segment, but it does not control for type-specific surfaces. Masterpieces require large walls and more time. Tags require accessible, high-traffic surfaces that allow quick placement. If these surface types are spatially concentrated, types may separate for opportunity reasons rather than writer-to-writer competition. Differential removal, enforcement, career-stage differences [@macdonald2001; @vanloon2014], and residual spatial dependence may also contribute to the pattern, even after the constrained checks in Appendix 6. Future research should include environmental covariates such as wall availability, pedestrian flow, and surveillance density. This would clarify how much of the lower co-presence reflects competitive sorting rather than opportunity.

A third limitation is classification error. Each graffiti instance was coded by one observer. We therefore could not calculate inter-rater reliability from the original data. We treated classification error as a sensitivity issue. STROBE recommends that observational studies discuss possible sources of bias [@vandenbroucke2007]. The misclassification check (Appendix 9) follows guidance on quantitative bias analysis [@lash2014] and is consistent with reviews of its use in observational research [@petersen2021]. In this check, we simulated plausible graffiti-type coding errors and recomputed the log(x + 1) MHI and Fixed-Fixed test. The MHI values changed. In some scenarios, the observed values moved closer to the null expectation. The main conclusion did not change. Observed co-presence remained lower than the null expectation, and all main pairwise comparisons remained significant at *p* `r alpha_05`. This does not show that classification error was absent. It shows only that plausible coding error is unlikely to explain the main finding. Future research should use multiple observers on the same graffiti instances to measure coding reliability directly.

# Conclusion

We examined whether graffiti types with contrasting levels of visual complexity are less likely to be co-present within street segments than expected under spatial indifference. In Ghent, observed co-presence was lower than the Fixed-Fixed null expectation for all three pairwise comparisons. The result was strongest for the most visually contrasting pair and weakest for the least contrasting pair. The main contribution is to show that graffiti location choice is not only about finding visible places. It also appears to involve the visual composition of those places. We therefore extend spot theory from absolute visibility to relative visibility. Absolute visibility depends on exposure to potential audiences. Relative visibility also depends on the graffiti already present at the same location. Because we use aggregate street-segment data, the findings should be read as pattern-level evidence. They are consistent with visibility-based competition, but they are not direct evidence of writer intent. Future longitudinal, ethnographic, and multi-city research can test whether similar co-presence patterns appear in other urban contexts.

# References

::: {#refs}
:::
